primer.markdown:
# Run MARS end-to-end on a stuck system you actually own

You have now seen the shape: parallel fan-out, epistemic constraint classification, systems-level metrics, five continuous architect gates. The MARS Quick Reference packages that whole arc as six concrete operations — and this module turns them back at you.

The six operations are commands you can call by name: `/mars research <problem>` for multi-domain parallel discovery, `/mars synthesize <research>` to integrate into a unified framework, `/mars apply <blueprint>` to map the framework to your real-world context, `/mars optimize <system>` to find leverage points, `/mars validate <blueprint>` to test against constraints, and `/mars iterate <results>` to learn and improve. Those six, in that order, are the full end-to-end run.

The Quick Reference is also explicit about when *not* to invoke MARS. `Don't use MARS when: Single domain sufficient (use specialized agent instead); Simple tactical execution (use Task Relay); Pure research needed (use deep-researcher); Code implementation (use practical-programmer).` MARS is for the multi-domain, systems-level, organizational-transformation class of problem — festival operations, manufacturing transformation, emergency-department throughput. If your stuck system fits that silhouette, it qualifies.

This module is the canonical last module of the quest. You pick one genuinely stuck system in your world — a process, an organization, a product line, a system that has fought every attempt at fixing it — and you run the six MARS operations on it end to end. You publish the analysis with sources cited. You get back a permalink you can hand to someone else. That is the sovereignty clause: you own what you ran, and you own what the run produced.
primer.key_insight: MARS ships as six named operations — research, synthesize, apply, optimize, validate, iterate — invoked in that order on a stuck system you own.

visual.alt: Six-step pipeline diagram, labeled left to right: research → synthesize → apply → optimize → validate → iterate. Each step is an octahedron node with a token-budget annotation (30K, 15K, 10K, 12K, 8K, 10K). A final output ribbon on the right is stamped with the learner's handle and a permalink URL.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner pastes a one-sentence description of a stuck system they own into the form, selects at least three candidate domains from the decomposition list, and submits. Phase 1 stores the submission locally and marks the module complete; Phase 3 will run the six-operation pipeline live and return a permalink to the generated analysis.

artifact.excerpt:
## The 6 Operations

```bash
/mars research <problem>        # Multi-domain parallel discovery
/mars synthesize <research>     # Integrate into unified framework
/mars apply <blueprint>         # Map to real-world context
/mars optimize <system>         # Find leverage points
/mars validate <blueprint>      # Test against constraints
/mars iterate <results>         # Learn and improve
```

questions.prompt: Per the MARS Quick Reference, which sequence lists the six core operations in the order an end-to-end run invokes them?
questions.explanation: MARS_QUICK_REFERENCE.md §The 6 Operations (lines 27-35) lists them in this exact order: research, synthesize, apply, optimize, validate, iterate. Research gathers the parallel fan-out; synthesis integrates it; apply grounds it in your context; optimize finds leverage; validate tests against constraints; iterate captures learnings for the next run.

questions.prompt: The Quick Reference lists four cases where you should NOT use MARS. Which of the following is one of them?
questions.explanation: MARS_QUICK_REFERENCE.md §When to Use MARS (lines 18-23) states: `Don't use MARS when: Single domain sufficient (use specialized agent instead); Simple tactical execution (use Task Relay); Pure research needed (use deep-researcher); Code implementation (use practical-programmer).` Single-domain problems belong to specialized agents; MARS is for multi-domain, systems-level work.

questions.prompt: You are about to run the six MARS operations on a stuck system you own. What kind of problem does the Quick Reference say actually qualifies?
questions.explanation: MARS_QUICK_REFERENCE.md §When to Use MARS (lines 11-16) enumerates the positive cases: `Challenge requires multi-domain analysis (logistics + tech + people + finance); Need systems-level intelligence; Organizational transformation required; SpaceX-level innovation desired; Complex operations (festival, manufacturing, product launch, transformation).` The multi-domain, systems-level silhouette is the qualifier.
